text: The patient is positioned in a lithotomy position, with the perineal region exposed using traction sutures. A wide transverse incision is made in the perineal skin, displaying a slight inferior concavity.

The surgeon performs a meticulous dissection of the perineal body until reaching the fistula tract, carefully avoiding rectal perforation. The procedure involves using a finger inside the anal canal for guidance. The anovaginal fistula tract is sectioned with scissors, revealing a fistula orifice in the anal canal. Blunt dissection of the rectovaginal septum continues, followed by resection of the vaginal fistula orifice and trimming of the rectal fistula orifice edges.

The surgeon performs a primary closure using simple sutures from top to bottom. The anterior rectal wall is then applied to provide additional support to the sutures and enhance the basal pressure tone in the anal canal.

The surgeon minimally dissects the retracted ends of the external anal sphincter, ensuring they can overlap without causing nerve damage. The sphincteroplasty is completed by overlapping one end above the other to address severe anal incontinence.

The surgeon performs an elliptical skin incision on the right outer lip, initiating the mobilization of a flap that includes the bulbocavernous muscle and surrounding adipose tissue. This flap is prepared for interposition in the hyaluron vaginal space.

The surgeon carefully separates the lateral and superior parts of the flap, detaching it posteriorly while preserving the posterior inferior vascular pedicle supplied by the internal pudendal artery. The liberated flap is interposed between the posterior vaginal wall and the anal canal, with the skin of the flap intact for reconstructing the posterior vaginal wall and perineum. The skin edge of the flap is sutured to the posterior wall of the vagina.
The surgeon sutures the flap to close the perineal defect, followed by closing the wound in the outer lip.
